canto_title: The Encounter with Statius and the Metaphysics of the Soul

scenes.summary: Dante describes the setting of the journey and the arduous climb up the path.
Dante compares his own conflicted desire to a young stork reluctant to leave its nest.
Virgil prompts Dante to release his question like an arrow from a bow.
Dante asks how life can be sustained where there is no nourishment.
Virgil responds by introducing Statius as a healer for Dante's intellectual struggle.
Statius agrees to answer the question if Dante is able to follow his logic.
Statius begins his lecture by explaining the formative and nutritive power of blood.
Statius describes the physiological process of nutrients being processed and transformed within the body.
Statius explains the transition from simple plant-like life to the complex intellect of animals.
Statius describes how the primary motor animates the brain to create a self-aware soul.
Statius uses the metaphor of the thread of life and the sharpening of the faculties at death.
Statius describes how the formative virtue radiates through all the limbs of the body.

scenes.scene_name: The Metaphor of Light and Shadow
scenes.summary: Statius explains how the soul's form is shaped by light and shadow, much like colored air.

scenes.scene_name: The Cause of the Souls' Cries
scenes.summary: Statius concludes by explaining that the souls' sighs and tears are shaped by their earthly desires.
The travelers encounter a dangerous, flaming edge of the pit and struggle to stay on the path.
Virgil warns Dante to keep a tight grip on his focus to avoid falling into the flames.
The travelers witness spirits moving through the flames while singing a hymn to God's mercy.
The sacred hymn is interrupted by cries referencing the myths of Diana and Elice.
Men and women cry out in a manner that honors the virtues of chastity and marriage.
Dante concludes that this rhythmic chanting serves as the only sustenance for the burning souls.
